Anu Gupta
Case Study
Saudia Airlines · Self-Booking Tool

Designing
smarter features
within an enterprise
design system

Adding travel policy intelligence, preferred time controls, and flexible booking roles to Saudia's corporate SBT — without breaking what already worked.

RoleUI/UX Designer
PlatformWeb · Mobile
ToolsFigma
DomainCorporate Travel SaaS
Overview

Project context

Saudia Airlines operates a corporate Self-Booking Tool (SBT) used by employees and travel managers to plan and book business travel. The product already had an established design system — our task was not to redesign it, but to extend it with new features that addressed real gaps in the corporate booking flow.

The challenge: every addition had to feel native to Saudia's existing visual language — the same typography, spacing tokens, green brand palette, and component patterns — while solving genuinely new interaction problems.

My Contribution
Feature design within existing design system. Figma design for web and mobile.
Key Features Added
Preferred time selector · Book As · Trip category · Policy & preference on flight card
Constraint
All components had to conform to Saudia's established design system — zero visual drift.
The Problem

What the original flow was missing

Corporate travel booking is fundamentally different from consumer booking. Employees travel within company policies, sometimes book on behalf of colleagues, and need to distinguish between personal and business trips. The original Saudia SBT flow treated all of these as a generic booking — creating friction, policy violations, and repeated manual corrections downstream.

Corporate travel managers reported that employees frequently missed preferred travel windows, booked outside policy without realising it, and had no way to indicate whether they were booking for themselves or as a delegate — leading to approval delays and invoice mismatches.

Features Designed

Four targeted additions to the booking flow

Preferred Time (Departure)

Employees set a preferred departure time with a ±hour flexibility range, reducing search noise and surfacing the most relevant flights first.

Preferred Time (Return)

Same time-range control applied to return journey — independently configurable so round trips don't force symmetric schedules.

Book As (Role Selector)

Employees select whether they are booking as themselves (Employee) or as a delegate for another traveller — unlocking the correct approval chain.

Trip Category

A "Business trip / Personal" toggle lets the system apply the right expense category, policy rules, and approval workflow automatically — no manual tagging after booking.

Policy & Preference on Flight Card

Each flight result in the departure list now surfaces two contextual signals inline: a preference score (e.g. "90% preference", "30% preference") derived from past booking patterns, and a policy indicator that flags whether a flight falls within the company's allowed fare class and timing windows. Travellers make informed choices without leaving the list view.

Design Work

Screens in context

Both screens show how new features were embedded into Saudia's existing component library — same card treatment, same typography scale, same green token system.

Search Form — Book As & Trip Category

New fields added to the existing search modal without increasing visual complexity. The "Book as and trip category" dropdown uses Saudia's standard select component; the preferred time fields extend the date row pattern already in the system.

1
"Book as and trip category" — new dropdown combining role + category in one field to reduce form length
2
Preferred time ± hrs — inline time flexibility picker added to both departure and return date rows
3
"What is this?" help links — non-intrusive disclosure pattern for new concepts

Departure List — Preference & Policy Signals

Each flight card was extended to carry preference and policy data without restructuring the card layout. A colour-coded badge and short label communicate compliance status at a glance.

1
Preference % badge — derived from company booking history; helps employees align with organisational norms
2
Policy filter in sort bar — allows travel managers to surface only within-policy options
3
Date strip with pricing — unchanged Saudia pattern; new features don't interfere with core navigation
Design Decisions

Key constraints and how I navigated them

1
ChallengeAdding 4 new fields to the search form without increasing cognitive load or breaking the layout rhythm.
DecisionMerged "Book as" and "Trip category" into a single combined dropdown — matching the existing "Passengers and class" field pattern. Preferred time controls were embedded inline within the date row rather than added as separate rows.
2
ChallengeSurfacing policy compliance on the flight card without cluttering a data-dense list view.
DecisionUsed a minimal badge below the departure city — same location across all cards — so the eye learns where to look. Badge colour maps to Saudia's existing status token (green = preferred, red = attention), keeping it system-native.
3
ChallengeIntroducing concepts ("Book as", "Trip category") that were new to most employees.
DecisionAdded a subtle "What is this?" disclosure link beneath each new field — a zero-friction way to access a tooltip or modal explanation without cluttering the label space.
4
ChallengeMaintaining design system fidelity across web and mobile variants.
DecisionAll new components were built from Saudia's existing atoms — no new colours, radii, or type styles were introduced. Mobile layouts stack the date + preferred time rows vertically, following the system's established responsive pattern.
Outcome

Impact of the additions

The features were designed to reduce policy friction, improve booking accuracy, and give employees clearer context before selecting a flight — without requiring any re-training on the core booking flow.

↓ Manual
Fewer manual trip-category corrections after booking, as classification happens at search time
↑ Policy
Inline compliance signals allow travellers to self-correct before selecting out-of-policy flights
0 Drift
All additions passed design system review — no new tokens, no visual inconsistency introduced
Reflection

What I learnt from constraint-based design

Working within Saudia's design system was a different kind of design challenge — the question was never "what should this look like?" but "where does this fit within what already exists?" That constraint forced more precise thinking: every new element had to earn its place by solving a real problem, not by introducing something visually novel. The most satisfying decisions were the ones that made the addition feel inevitable — as if it had always been part of the product.
